title: Accessing Arrays
description: Read and change specific items in an array list.

slides.title: Accessing Array Elements 👁️
slides.content: You access an array element by referring to its **index number** inside square brackets `[]`.

slides.title: Zero-Indexed Data 0️⃣
slides.content:
However, array indices start with 0.

`[0]` is the first element. `[1]` is the second element, etc.

This is a huge source of bugs for beginners, so remember: computers start counting at ZERO!

slides.title: Example
slides.codeSnippet:
string cars[4] = {"Volvo", "BMW", "Ford", "Mazda"};
cout << cars[0];  // Prints Volvo

quizData.question: In the array above, how do you grab 'Ford'?
quizData.explanation: Volvo is 0, BMW is 1, and Ford is index 2.

slides.title: Changing Array Elements 🔄
slides.content: To change the value of a specific element, refer to the index number and assign it a new value.

slides.title: Example
slides.content: Changing the first element from Volvo to Opel.

quizData.question: cars[4] will output 'Mazda' since Mazda is the 4th item.
quizData.explanation: False! Because indexing starts at 0, the items are [0], [1], [2], [3]. If you ask for [4], you will get an Out-of-Bounds memory error!
